name: Things That Think (TTT)
short_description: An MIT Media Lab research consortium founded in 1995 to explore moving computation out of conventional computers and into everyday objects.
long_description: Things That Think (TTT) was a sponsored research consortium at the MIT Media Lab, initiated in 1995 and active through 2014, that brought together dozens of companies and research groups around a single vision: computation and communication migrating out of PCs and into ordinary objects like toasters, doorknobs, shoes, and coffee mugs. Co-directed by Neil Gershenfeld and articulated in his 1999 book 'When Things Start to Think', it framed everyday objects sensing their owners, learning habits, and communicating with one another to solve real problems. As a foundational vision for ubiquitous computing and tangible interaction, TTT matters to physical AI as an early, influential articulation of intelligence dissolving into the material world rather than living on a screen.
ai_note: TTT is a research vision and consortium for embedding intelligence into everyday objects; it framed the concept of objects that sense and learn rather than shipping a single implemented AI system.

images.caption: Things That Think consortium page at the MIT Media Lab, framing the migration of computation into everyday objects.
images.alt_text: MIT Media Lab Things That Think consortium web page describing digitally augmented objects and environments.

interactions.title: Your shoe announces who you are as you walk
interactions.description: A piezoelectric insole harvests power from each footfall and uses it to broadcast a 12-bit RFID identifier, so simply walking across a room transmits your ID with no battery to charge.
interactions.source_label: MIT Media Lab — Parasitic Power in Shoes

interactions.title: A handshake passes a business card through your body
interactions.description: Wearing a Personal Area Network transmitter, you shake someone's hand and a digital business card flows through the tiny electric fields coupled across both bodies, arriving in their device without a wire or radio.

interactions.title: Badges that read the room's agreement
interactions.description: At lab meetings, attendees wore sensor badges that gauged 'intellectual proximity' between people and lit colored bars to show agreement; within minutes participants reinvented the handshake, exposing their chests to swap information.
interactions.source_label: Salon — Have my shoe talk to your refrigerator

interactions.title: A cello that enlarges its own sound space
interactions.description: An instrument augmented with sensors lets a cellist like Yo-Yo Ma control more sounds in more ways, the bow and fingering driving computation that expands the sonic palette beyond the strings alone.
interactions.source_label: Salon — Have my shoe talk to your refrigerator

interactions.title: Coming home, your shoes brief the house
interactions.description: Before you take off your coat, the computing in your shoes talks to the carpet, which cues delivery of the day's personalized news to your glasses — the consortium's vision of everyday objects relaying you to one another.
interactions.source_label: MIT Media Lab — Things That Think

interactions.title: A power-generating shoe on museum display
interactions.description: An early shoe-mounted rotary magnetic generator that powered a portable radio as the wearer walked was exhibited at the London Museum of Science and Industry, putting the parasitic-power idea in front of the public.
interactions.source_label: MIT Media Lab — Parasitic Power in Shoes

interactions.title: Grab a doorknob and unlock by touch alone
interactions.description: Building on body-coupled signaling, a doorknob or transit gate could read the credential a person already carries in their shoe the instant skin meets metal, with no card to tap or phone to wake.

interactions.title: A walked-in charge for the device you forgot to plug in
interactions.description: The same footfall harvesting that powered an RFID beacon could top up a hearing aid or a low-energy sensor over the course of a stroll, so the act of getting somewhere quietly refuels what you carry.
